original_abstract: While the church is divinely commissioned to express God's love to all global ethnic groups, contemporary cross-cultural mission activities (*missio ecclesiae*) are often narrowly defined. Influenced heavily by the Great Commission in Matthew 28:19–20, many Evangelical and Pentecostal traditions limit their missionary focus primarily to verbal evangelism. However, a broader biblical perspective suggests that local church involvement should be far more expansive. Through a missiological thematic analysis of Romans 15:22–33, this study demonstrates how the church can "share its power" and resources in three holistic ways: by providing logistical and financial support for field evangelism, by funding humanitarian and diaconal services for marginalized communities, and by engaging in disciplined prayer for ongoing tasks. Ultimately, this research argues that authentic mission work is holistic, necessitating a commitment to both the spiritual and physical well-being of the global community.
humanized_abstract: While the church, as the body of Christ, is divinely mandated to demonstrate God’s love to all the ethnic groups of the world, the practice of cross-cultural mission as a lived reality of *missio ecclesiae* is often reduced to the minimum. Often concentrated towards the imperative of the Great Commission in Matthew 28:19–20 and focusing therefore primarily on evangelism, the New Testament grounds for Evangelical and Pentecostal practices of local church missionary involvement are limited. Using a missiological thematic analysis of Romans 15:22–33 this research study highlights the ways in which the church, empowered by God, can share its power and means to accomplish the tasks of world evangelisation in the following three ways: 1) by underpinning or supporting the evangelism activities that are sent to the mission field; 2) by offering a platform through which the various Diaconal Works that can be rendered to the local communities on the mission field can be supported; 3) through praying to God for the on-going missionary activities that stretch across space and time. Overall, the task of mission cannot be reduced to just spiritual or just economic and physical terms. Mission needs to be whole and comprehensive in order to bring in the total presence and care of God to meet all the needs that people may have.
